title: Localization of Active Particles on Random Arrays of Parallel Filaments
abstract: Quenched disorder in the environment can fundamentally alter transport dynamics in both active and passive systems. We explore how disordered arrays of filaments govern the distribution of intermittently moving particles which switch between diffusive and processive transport. Motivated by the mixed-polarity arrangements of parallel microtubules observed in mammalian dendrites, we show that such arrays tend to result in localization of particles at regions of convergent filament orientation. In the rapid attachment-detachment limit, the disordered system can be described by a noisy one-dimensional effective energy landscape, whose structure is approximated by a random walk. The depth and width of wells on this landscape are expressed as a function of the transport kinetics and system geometry. Localization is shown to be strongest at intermediate run-lengths, where biased transport persists long enough to sense the quenched filament polarity but not so long as to facilitate escape from local traps. These results demonstrate robust localization of particles moving on random filament networks, highlighting the emergent spatial organization that arises from an interplay of active transport and quenched disorder.
summary: This work analyzes particle localization on random arrays of filaments, drawing inspiration from biological microtubules. By modeling the system using an advection-diffusion equation, the authors show that particle accumulation (localization) is robust and strongest when the particle's active transport length is intermediate. This highlights how active processes interact with quenched disorder to create stable spatial organization.
main_problem: The paper investigates how disordered arrays of filaments govern the distribution of intermittently moving particles that switch between diffusive and processive transport.
main_result: The system exhibits robust particle localization, which is strongest for intermediate run-lengths, demonstrating emergent spatial organization from the interplay of active transport and quenched disorder.
method: The analysis models the system using an advection-diffusion equation derived from the rapid attachment-detachment limit, mapping the steady-state density onto an effective energy landscape.
model_or_system: The physical system is a 1D random array of parallel filaments, motivated by mixed-polarity microtubules in dendrites. Particles switch between passive diffusion and active, biased motion along these fixed filaments.
key_observables: Localization strength, quantified by the Inverse Participation Ratio (IPR), and the resulting particle density profile $ho_i(x) \propto e^{-\epsilon_i(x)}$.
important_parameters: Diffusivity ($D$), attachment/detachment rates ($k_a, k_d$), bias parameter ($b$), and the particle run-length ($\lambda_v$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on a 'single-layer' approximation, assuming attachment/detachment rates are much faster than filament traversal times, and the system is analyzed in a 1D axial coordinate.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate simulation snapshots of biased particles, the schematic of motility state transitions, the resulting energy landscape, and plots showing the condition for tight localization based on kinetic parameters.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the physical model and the governing advection-diffusion equation. It then derives the effective energy landscape and analyzes the conditions (intermediate run-lengths) under which localization is maximized, providing quantitative diagnostics like the IPR.
why_interesting: The framework of mapping transport dynamics onto an effective energy landscape due to quenched disorder is highly relevant to understanding energy barriers and trapping mechanisms in complex quantum or classical systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Simulation snapshot of biased particles (orange) navigating a parallel filament array, with filament plus ends pointing right (red) or left (blue). Only the axial dimension is resolved in the simulations. (b) Inset: schematic of transitions between motility states. Histogram: steady-state distribution from simulations, Line: analytic distribution from Eq. 3. (c) Region decomposition of the filament array, showing effective velocity (arrows) and diffusivity (black curve) in each region. (d) Energy landscape corresponding to the filament configu- ration. Parameters used: Lf = 1µm, n = 7, Ldom = 7µm, v = 1µm, D = 0.1µm2, ka = kd = 100s−1, b = 1.

FIG. 2. (a) Steady-state localization width (IPR−1, color), plotted as a function of diffusive length λD and run length λv. Black curve tracks values where the energy landscape range (Eq. 7) reaches R = 5, marking a transition to tight localization. (b) Steady-state distribution of particles on a sample filament configuration for two different run lengths (λv = 0.02µm, 0.2µm). (c) Energy landscapes (ϵi) corre- sponding to the distributions in (b) are shown in blue and red. The potential function Ui (green and brown) provides a smoothed estimate of the landscape. Parameters used: Lf = 1µm, n = 40, ka = kd = 100s−1, b = 1 and Ldom = 100µm in (c). IPR−1 color plot is averaged over 200...

FIG. 4. Localization width, quantified by IPR−1, is plot- ted versus (a) particle velocity and (b) filament length. Black dots: mean values from 200 configurations, with error bars in- dicating standard deviation. Solid red and dashed blue curves show analytic approximations for the W ≫Lf (Eq. 8) and W ≪Lf (Eq. 9), respectively.

FIG. 3. Depth of energy landscape collapses to a universal curve as a function of the ratio of length scales λgeom/λkin. Inset illustrates the landscape range R, well width W, and correlated step size s for a truncated energy landscape, with red dots marking region boundaries. Dots show range of the bulk landscape ϵ, averaged over 200 sampled configurations, while kinetic and geometric parameters are varied. Dashed line gives analytic prediction from Eq. 7. Default param- eter values: b = 1, v = 1µm, D = 0.1µ2m/s, ka = kd = 100s−1, n = 40, Ldom = 100µm, Lf = 1µm. Edge regions within distance Lf of the domain boundaries are excluded from the calculation.
